title: Vercel vs AWS for Small Business Apps: My Honest Take
description: Trying to decide between Vercel and AWS for your small business app? Here's what actually broke on AWS, what Vercel fixed, and which one I would pick.
keywords: best hosting for small business apps
bannerAlt: Vercel vs AWS comparison diagram for small business apps showing hosting architecture side by side

body.text: Vercel vs AWS: Why This Decision Trips People Up

If you have spent an evening reading Vercel vs AWS threads and come away more confused than when you started, that is normal. Both platforms market themselves as the easy button for shipping a web app, and on the surface their pitches sound almost identical. They are not solving the same problem.

Vercel is a hosting platform built around one job: deploying frontend frameworks, especially Next.js, with almost no configuration. AWS is a full cloud provider with over 200 services, and hosting a web app is just one small slice of what it does. Comparing them head to head only makes sense once you know which slice of AWS you would actually be using, because raw AWS with no framework is a different animal than something like Vercel.

I ended up thinking hard about this comparison again recently after inheriting a client's Next.js app that was running on a fairly typical DIY AWS setup: an EC2 box running the server, an S3 bucket for uploads, and CloudFront sitting in front of it. One slow month, with maybe 400 total users, the AWS bill came in at 340 dollars. Nobody on the client's side knew why, and honestly neither did I until I opened the billing console and started digging line by line.

I moved the frontend to Vercel over a single afternoon. First deploy took about 20 minutes, including DNS. On the old setup, pushing a change meant SSHing in, pulling the repo, rebuilding, and manually invalidating CloudFront if static assets changed. That difference alone made the case. But I will be upfront about the trade off: a background job that generated PDF invoices needed longer execution time than Vercel's serverless functions allow on the plan we were using, so that piece stayed on AWS Lambda. This is not a clean either or story, and anyone who tells you it is has probably not run both in production.
Key Takeaways

items: Vercel wins on setup speed and zero config hosting if your app is Next.js.
AWS wins when you need fine grained control, long running jobs, or specific compliance requirements.
Vercel's pricing gets less predictable once you cross into serious bandwidth or function usage.
AWS costs less at scale but only if someone is actively managing it.
Most small business apps do not need AWS's full power on day one, and a hybrid setup is common.

body.text: What Vercel Actually Gets You Right
Zero config deploys built around Next.js

Vercel is built by the same team behind Next.js, so features like the App Router, Incremental Static Regeneration, and edge middleware just work without extra configuration. You connect a GitHub repo, push to main, and it builds and deploys automatically, with a unique preview URL for every pull request. For a small team without a dedicated DevOps person, that preview URL workflow alone saves real time during client review cycles.
Where Vercel costs can sneak up on you

The Hobby plan is free but not meant for commercial use, and the Pro plan starts at 20 dollars a month per user with usage based charges layered on top for bandwidth, function execution time, and image optimization. A marketing site with a few thousand visits costs nothing extra. An app doing heavy server side rendering on every request, or serving large video files, can push past the included limits faster than people expect. Vercel documents this directly in its pricing and usage documentation, and it is worth reading before you commit a client to the platform.
What AWS Actually Gets You Right
Full control, if you actually want to own the ops

AWS gives you EC2, ECS, Lambda, RDS, and dozens of other services you can combine however you want. You choose the region, the instance size, the network setup, and you get direct access to compliance certifications that matter for healthcare, finance, or government adjacent clients. Nothing about Vercel stops you from being compliant, but AWS puts the controls directly in your hands rather than behind a managed layer.
AWS Amplify as the middle ground

AWS Amplify Hosting tries to close the gap with Vercel by offering git based deploys and a simplified build pipeline on top of standard AWS infrastructure. It is a reasonable option if you already have other services running on AWS and want your frontend on the same billing account and IAM setup. In my experience the developer experience is still a step behind Vercel's, particularly around build speed and preview environments, but Amazon's own Amplify Hosting documentation covers the current feature set if you want to check for yourself.
Vercel vs AWS at a Glance

rows: Next.js apps, marketing sites, small dashboards
Teams that need full infrastructure control
Teams already committed to AWS services
Apps with long running tasks or file processing
body.caption: A rough comparison based on setups I have actually deployed for small business clients

body.text: A Quick Look at the Deploy Difference

The clearest way to see the gap is to look at what each platform actually asks you to write. On Vercel, deploying a Next.js app from the command line is a single command once the project is linked. On AWS, even a simple Lambda function behind an API Gateway needs its own infrastructure definition, something like this SAM template for a background job.

body.code:
# template.yaml - minimal AWS SAM setup for a background job
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Transform: AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31
Resources:
  InvoicePdfFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      Handler: handler.generateInvoice
      Runtime: nodejs20.x
      Timeout: 60
      MemorySize: 512
      Events:
        InvoiceQueue:
          Type: SQS
          Properties:
            Queue: !GetAtt InvoiceQueue.Arn
  InvoiceQueue:
    Type: AWS::SQS::Queue
    Properties:
      VisibilityTimeout: 90

# compare that to deploying the Next.js frontend on Vercel:
# vercel --prod

body.text: Neither approach is wrong. The SAM template gives you precise control over timeout, memory, and the queue that triggers the function, which matters for a job that takes longer than Vercel's function limits allow. The single command gives you speed when that level of control is not the bottleneck.
When I Would Actually Pick Each One

If you are launching a new small business app and traffic is under roughly 50,000 visits a month, start on Vercel. The time you save on deployment and DNS setup is worth more early on than any cost difference, and you can always add AWS services later for specific jobs.

Pick AWS from the start if you already know you need something Vercel does not offer well: long running background processing, a specific compliance certification, direct database hosting inside a private VPC, or an existing AWS account with other services you need to integrate against.

Go hybrid, the way the app I mentioned earlier ended up, when your frontend is straightforward Next.js but a specific feature, PDF generation, video processing, scheduled reports, needs more execution time or memory than Vercel functions allow. This is the setup I build most often once a client's app matures past its first few months.
The AWS Bill That Taught Me a Lesson

Early in my freelance work I set up a client's staging and production environments in separate VPCs on AWS, each with its own NAT gateway so private subnets could reach the internet for package updates. I did not fully account for NAT gateway data processing charges, which bill per gigabyte regardless of whether the traffic is meaningful. Two idle staging environments quietly ran up close to 90 dollars in a month just from routine health checks and log shipping.

The fix was simple once I found it: share a single NAT gateway across non production environments and only isolate things that actually needed isolation. But it taught me to check AWS's VPC pricing page line by line before assuming a security best practice is free. On Vercel, this specific problem simply does not exist, because there is no VPC to misconfigure in the first place.
My Actual Recommendation for Most Small Business Apps

For the majority of small business apps I build, Vercel is where the frontend lives, and AWS shows up only for the specific pieces that need it: file storage in S3, a queue worker in Lambda, maybe RDS if the data model genuinely needs a managed relational database. That is what I set up as part of web app development, API and Backend Development for most clients now, because it matches the effort to the actual complexity of the app instead of over engineering from day one.

If you are not sure which category your app falls into yet, that is a normal place to be. I usually spend the first conversation just mapping out what the app actually needs to do before recommending infrastructure, since guessing wrong here is expensive to unwind later. I also write about the deployment and DNS side of running these apps over on the blog, including the email delivery setup that usually comes up right after hosting.
FAQ
Is Vercel more expensive than AWS?

For low traffic apps, Vercel is usually cheaper once you factor in engineering time. AWS can be cheaper at real scale, but only if someone is actively optimizing it. Most small business apps never reach the traffic level where that optimization pays for itself.
Can I use AWS services alongside Vercel?

Yes, this is common. Many teams host their Next.js frontend on Vercel while using AWS S3 for file storage, Lambda for background jobs, or RDS for a managed database. The two are not mutually exclusive and often work well together.
Is AWS Amplify a good alternative to Vercel?

It is a reasonable option if you are already committed to AWS for other reasons, like compliance or existing services. For a standalone Next.js app with no other AWS dependencies, Vercel generally offers a smoother build and deploy experience.
Does AWS make more sense for compliance heavy apps?

Often yes. AWS gives direct control over region, network isolation, and specific certifications that some industries require. Vercel can still support compliant setups, but AWS puts more of that configuration directly in your hands rather than behind a managed platform.
